1: ballaylāh hahûʾ nāḏəḏāh šənaṯ hammeleḵ — 'On that night the sleep of the king fled' (nāḏaḏ, H5074 = to flee, stray; used elsewhere for fugitives and scattered birds). The subject is the abstract noun šənāt (sleep, H8142) — in the idiom of biblical narrative the sleep itself becomes the subject that flees, as if animated. The rabbis read this as the sleep of God himself (Midrash Esther Rabbah): 'the Sleep of the King of the Universe fled.' Without naming divine agency, the narrator uses the passive description of a king's insomnia at precisely the critical moment — the night before Haman plans to request Mordecai's execution — to signal providential timing. wayyōʾmer lhāḇîʾ ʾeṯ-sēfer hazziḵrōnôṯ diḇrê hayyāmîm — 'and he commanded that the book of records of the chronicles (sēfer hazziḵrōnôṯ diḇrê hayyāmîm) be brought.' The term zikkārôn (H2146 = memorial, record, that which calls to mind) designates the court chronicles that preserve the memory of significant acts. The same root as Mordecai's unrecorded (until now) act of loyalty.
2: wayyimmāṣēʾ kāṯûḇ — 'and it was found written' (māṣāʾ, H4672, niphal = to be found; the passive emphasizes that no human directed the search to this particular entry). The passive construction is characteristic of Esther's narrative theology: things are 'found,' appointments 'happen,' the king 'could not sleep' — agency is never attributed to human calculation or divine intervention but to a series of passive events that accumulate. What is found written is the specific account of Mordecai's report of Bigthana and Teresh's assassination plot (Esther 2:21-23). ʾăšer higgîḏ Mordoḵay ʿal bignəṯānāʾ wāṯereš — 'that Mordecai had informed about Bigthana and Teresh.' The pluperfect construction situates this as a past act now being retrieved from institutional memory.
3: māh-naʿăśāh yəqār ûgəḏûllāh lə-Mordoḵay ʿal-zeh — 'What honor and dignity was given (naʿăśāh = was done) to Mordecai for this?' The question is the pivot of the narrative. Yəqār (H3366 = honor, preciousness, worth) and gəḏûllāh (H1420 = greatness, magnitude) together constitute the Persian court vocabulary for official recognition and advancement. The king's attendants answer: lōʾ-naʿăśāh ʿimmô dāḇār — 'nothing was done for him.' The gap between the service rendered (saving the king's life) and the reward received (nothing) is the narrative engine: the unrecompensed good deed is about to be recompensed at exactly the wrong moment for Haman.
4: wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ mî baḥāṣēr — 'The king said, Who is in the court?' The king asks who is present at what must have been early morning (he has been awake all night reading records). wəHāmān bāʾ laḥăṣar bêṯ-hammeleḵ haḥîṣônāh — 'Haman had come to the outer court of the king's house.' The outer court (ḥāṣar haḥîṣônāh) is the public entry area. Haman is arriving precisely to request Mordecai's hanging on the gallows he constructed (Esther 5:14 — he had the 50-cubit gallows built that night). The timing: the king insomniac, records open to Mordecai's act, seeking someone to reward — and at that moment the man who intends to destroy Mordecai walks in. The Persian court's compression of coincidences is the book's theological signature.
5: wayyōmərû naʿărê hammeleḵ ʾēlāyw hinnēh Hāmān ʿōmēḏ baḥāṣēr — 'The king's servants said, Behold, Haman is standing in the court.' The deictic particle hinnēh (H2009 = behold, look) — a particle of personal and surprising observation — marks the coincidence as notable. wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ yāḇōʾ — 'The king said, Let him come in.' The immediate royal invitation gives Haman access before he can report his purpose — the narrative shows the king taking the initiative in a direction entirely opposite to Haman's.
6: wayyāḇōʾ Hāmān wayyōʾmer lô hammeleḵ māh laʿăśôṯ bāʾîš ʾăšer hammeleḵ ḥāpēṣ bîqārô — 'Haman came, and the king said to him, What should be done for the man whom the king delights to honor?' The king's question is utterly general — 'whom the king wishes to honor' — but the narrator immediately notes: wayyōʾmer Hāmān bəlibbô — 'Haman said in his heart' (bəlibbô, H3820 = in his heart/mind). The interior dialogue technique — showing Haman's unspoken reasoning — is the narrative's finest irony setup: ləmî yaḥpōṣ hammeleḵ laʿăśôṯ yəqār yôṯēr mimmennî — 'for whom would the king wish to honor more than me?' Haman's assumption of self-referentiality is the mechanism of his destruction: he designs the perfect honor he wishes for himself.
7: wayyōʾmer Hāmān ʾel-hammeleḵ — 'And Haman said to the king.' What follows is Haman's proposal of the honor ceremony, elaborated across vv7-9 as an increasingly extravagant display. The fourfold honor: the royal robe, the king's horse, a royal crown on the horse's head, and proclamation through the city square. ʾîš ʾăšer hammeleḵ ḥāpēṣ bîqārô — 'the man whom the king delights to honor' — Haman repeats the king's phrase, still (in his imagination) applying it to himself. The repetition of this phrase in vv6-11 (four times total) is the structural pattern of the reversal: each repetition shifts the referent from Haman's imagined self-application to Mordecai's actual receipt.
8: yāḇîʾû leḇûš malkûṯ ʾăšer lāḇaš-bô hammeleḵ — 'let royal robes be brought which the king has worn.' The request is for the actual garment the king wore, not a replica — an extraordinary honor that approximates royal identity. wəsûs ʾăšer rāḵaḇ ʿālāyw hammeleḵ — 'and a horse on which the king has ridden.' Again the actual royal horse, not a ceremonial equivalent. The parallelism of robe and horse — both ʾăšer + relative clause indicating the king's own — makes the honor the maximum possible short of actual kingship: to wear the king's robe on the king's horse is to impersonate the king in the public square.
9: wənaṯôn hallāḇûš wəhassûs ʿal-yaḏ-ʾîš miśśārê hammelek happartemîm — 'Let the robe and the horse be given into the hand of one of the king's most noble officials.' The term partemîm (H6579) is a Persian loanword designating the highest-ranking nobles, the inner circle of the court. Haman designs the honor to require a senior official to personally lead the horse through the streets — making the honor a public spectacle performed by the nobility itself. wihirqîḇuhû ʿal-hassûs birḥôḇ hāʿîr wəqārəʾû ləp̄ānāyw kākāh yēʿāśeh lāʾîš ʾăšer hammeleḵ ḥāpēṣ bîqārô — 'and they should proclaim before him: Thus is done for the man whom the king delights to honor.' The town-crier announcement turns the honor into public city-wide knowledge.
10: wayyōʾmer hammeleḵ lHāmān maher qaḥ ʾeṯ-hallāḇûš wəʾeṯ-hassûs — 'The king said to Haman: Quick, take the robe and the horse!' The adverb maher (H4116 = quickly, hurry) conveys royal urgency — the king is impatient to execute the plan. kaʾăšer dibbartā waʿăśēh-ḵēn ləMordoḵay hayyəhûḏî — 'as you have spoken, and do so to Mordecai the Jew.' The three words 'Mordecai the Jew' are the detonation of the irony: Haman designed the honor for himself; he must perform it for his designated victim. The title hayyəhûḏî (H3064 = the Jew) — the very identity marker that caused Haman's rage (Esther 3:4-6) — is placed immediately after Mordecai's name, making Haman serve the representative of the people he intended to destroy. ʾal-tappēl dāḇār mikkōl ʾăšer dibbartā — 'do not omit anything of all that you have spoken' — the king locks Haman into executing his own plan in full.
11: wayyiqqaḥ Hāmān ʾeṯ-hallāḇûš wəʾeṯ-hassûs — 'Haman took the robe and the horse.' The subject shift — Haman as the actor performing what he designed for himself upon Mordecai — is the structural center of the reversal narrative. wayyalbiš ʾeṯ-Mordoḵay wayyarkîḇēhû birḥôḇ hāʿîr wayyiqrāʾ ləp̄ānāyw kākāh yēʿāśeh lāʾîš ʾăšer hammeleḵ ḥāpēṣ bîqārô — 'He clothed Mordecai and had him ride through the city square, and proclaimed before him: Thus is done to the man whom the king delights to honor.' The proclamation formula is used for the fourth time — but now in full actuality, with Mordecai as recipient. The repetition is a rhetorical device of reversal: the same words, spoken by the same mouth, for the opposite man.
12: wayyāšāḇ Mordoḵay ʾel-šaʿar hammeleḵ — 'Mordecai returned to the king's gate.' The return to the gate — his post (Esther 2:19,21; Esther 3:2) — signals that Mordecai does not seek to leverage the honor into advancement. The gate (šaʿar, H8179) is where he belongs and where he returns. wHāmān nitdahar ʾel-bêṯô — 'Haman hurried to his house, mourning.' Nitdahar (H1765, niphal = to hasten, rush) and the participle ʾāḇēl (H57 = mourning, in grief) — the exact opposite of the public honor procession. The reversal is complete: Mordecai honored in public, Haman hiding at home in mourning.
13: wayyəsappēr Hāmān ləZereš ʾišṯô ûlə-ḵol-ʾōhăḇāyw ʾēṯ kol-ʾăšer qārāhû — 'Haman told his wife Zeresh and all his friends everything that had happened to him.' The same advisors who proposed the gallows (Esther 5:14) now deliver a counter-verdict. wayyōmərû lô ḥăḵāmāyw wəZereš ʾišṯô ʾim mizzareʿ hayyəhûḏîm Mordoḵay ʾăšer haḥillôṯā linpōl ləp̄ānāyw lōʾ-ṯûḵal lô wənāpôl tippōl ləp̄ānāyw — 'His advisors and Zeresh his wife said to him: If Mordecai before whom you have begun to fall is of Jewish descent, you will not overcome him but will fall before him.' The conditional sentence ʾim mizzereʿ hayyəhûḏîm (if from the seed of the Jews) implies that the Jews are recognized as a people under supernatural protection. Nāpôl tippōl (infinitive absolute + finite verb = you will certainly fall) is the emphatic construction. His own household pronounces the prophecy of his defeat.
14: ʿôḏām mədabbrîm ʿimmô — 'While they were still speaking with him' — the temporal clause creates urgency: before the counsel can be absorbed or responded to. wəsārîsê hammeleḵ higgîʿû wayyabhîlû lhāḇîʾ ʾeṯ-Hāmān ʾel-hammišṯeh ʾăšer ʿāśəṯāh ʾEsṯēr — 'the king's eunuchs arrived and hurried Haman to the banquet that Esther had prepared.' Bahel (H926, piel = to hurry, cause to hurry) — the same verb as v10's 'quick!' — urgency is the narrative rhythm of this chapter. Haman is pulled from ominous counsel to the banquet that will be his unraveling, without time to process what his household has just told him.
